full_text:
Reviewer comments and point-by-point responses

We thank the reviewers for their important insights on our work. We have now revised the manuscript and we have addressed all the comments, including an extensive restructuring of the figures, inclusion of supplemental data to the main figures, and edited numerous sections of the main text to improve the presentation of the data and to clarify the messages conveyed by our work.

The major changes to the work are:

Restructuring of the figures to greatly improve the display of main figures, and improved formatting of figures for better data visibility.

Inclusion of several panels of supplemental data into main figures to clarify presentation of results.

Inclusion of supporting information from prior murine model studies, and clinical trial data, answering the comments of the reviewers on justification of target selection and data set sizes.

Revised the main text to ensure improved integration of our manuscript findings to the existing literature.

Main novelties and impact of our study:

Humanized ex vivo model that recapitulates immune cell composition of source tumors, and can be used for preclinical testing of immunotherapy compounds on samples of both treatment-naïve and recurrence stages.

Display of discrete iPDC immune cell activation responses upon immunotherapy, and of cancer cell pathway activation in iPDCs in relation to clinical response status

Identification of replication stress biomarker, and spatial analysis on mechanics underlying ATRi response through effects on immune cell activation, confirming findings of previous studies and clinical trials.

Reviewer #1 (Comments to the Author ):

This paper by Nagaraj et al. is valuable in reporting that iPDCs using OmGel as an extracellular matrix can culture not only cancer cells but also immune cells ex vivo for 4-7 days, and can assess the efficacy of immune checkpoint inhibitors.

However, although various experiments have been conducted, the results are descriptive, and the verification of the final conclusion that cancers positive for pRPA32 in cancer cells have PD-1 CD8-positive T cells in the tumor vicinity and show high efficacy of ATR inhibitors is insufficient.

Author response: Thank you for highlighting this point. We acknowledge that our sample number is a limitation of the study. Our hypothesis of replication stress stemmed from our previous translational work on a randomized phase 2 (RP2) study (NCI-CTEP #9944, NCT02595892) investigating the efficacy of an ATR inhibitor in ovarian cancer https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-021-25904-w). In the translational analyses, we found that genomic alterations associated with replication stress significantly associate with responses to ATRi. Thus, in line with these clinical trial results, our iPDC model confirms the association of replication stress with responses to ATRi, and the spatial analyses extend beyond the current knowledge, showing significant associations also to the spatial interactions with immune cells.

We have now added more details about the clinical trial results, and the reference to the above study as a rationale of investigating pRPA32 in the spatial studies (lines 433-434). We have also edited the discussion to better convey that our results are in line with the clinical trial results, confirming the relevance of our preclinical iPDC model. We have also edited the discussion to fully acknowledge the limitations of our study (lines 485-487, 515-517).

The study demonstrates using iPDCs experiments that the efficacy of PD-1 inhibitors is high in HRD-positive cancers and low in HRP cancers. However, pRPA32, which is typically a replication stress marker, is higher in high-grade serous cancers with CCNE1 amplification (which accounts for 40% of HRP cancers) than in HRD ovarian cancers.

Author response: We thank the reviewer for this important distinction between HR status and CCNE1 amplification.

The correlation between HR status and PD-1 inhibitor response has been previously presented using the samples of the TOPACIO trial (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-020-15315-8). Our study thus confirms the result using our novel iPDC model, while providing additional insights into the spatial correlates in the source tumors that can facilitate the development and further testing of clinical biomarkers.

The use of pRPA32 expression as a predictor for ATRi response is not related to HR status or the PD-1 inhibition results, but as a biomarker for ATRi response as shown in the previous clinical trial translational work. In this translational work, we show that replication stress extends beyond CCNE1 amplification, and tumors commonly have other causes of replication stress (https://aacrjournals.org/cancerres/article/79/8/1730/640966/Exploiting-DNA-Replication-Stress-for-Cancer). In fact, in Konstantinopoulos et al, we showed that CCNE1 amplification accounts for only ~20 % of cases that exhibit replication stress, which correlated, also without CCNE1 amplification, with ATRi treatment response (https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-021-25904-w). Thus, we did not want to limit ourselves to CCNE1 amplification status, but rather use a more functional downstream marker that indicates replication stress resulting from different processes. Furthermore, only two cases in this study had CCNE1 amplification, C238 and C053, both of which are HRP. C238 did not respond to ATRi, and had lower than average pRPA32 levels, whereas C053 was an ATRi responder with slightly higher than average levels of pRPA32. These data further indicate the importance of factors outside CCNE1 amplification status in assessing replication stress.

According to the reviewer’s comment, we have now added a display of HR status to the revised Figure 4 panels B-F to clarify the genomic profile of the patients. Additionally, we have clarified the in the manuscript text with the rationale for using pRPA32 as a predictor of ATRi response (lines 425-427).

In the Figure 3 experiment, the relationship between pRPA32 and HRP is unclear and should be clarified, including the relationship with CCNE1 amplification or HRP.

Author response: We thank the reviewer for this comment, and have improved the text and figures to better represent that pRPA32-mediated replication stress is not directly linked to HR status. The revised Figure 4 panel F has been updated to contain HR status, and further rationalization between pRPA32 expression and ATRi response has been added (lines 425-427).

Overall, the results are based on analyses of small sample sizes, with insufficient validation of validity, making interpretation of the results difficult.

Author response: We thank the reviewer for this comment, and we acknowledge the small sample sizes. The results on the improved responses of HRD tumors to immunotherapies are well aligned with our previous work on higher immunogenicity and PD-L1 expression in HRD/BRCAmut tumors (https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-022-28389-3), and also with the results from the TOPACIO Phase II clinical trial showing improved PFS in HRD patients when combining PARPi with Pembrolizumab in platinum-resistant ovarian cancer (https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-020-15315-8). Moreover, our results on the ATRi are in line with the Phase II clinical trial showing the efficacy of ATRi in ovarian cancer (https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38635934/), which associate with a replication stress. In addition, we have previously published comprehensive mechanistic characterization in vivo on the effects of targeting the LPA pathway (https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/35552618/). The fact that our results using our iPDC model faithfully recapitulate results obtained from clinical trials and mouse models highlight the high relevance of our fully humanized iPDC model in testing new therapeutic agents for HGSC. We thus believe that our work offers a proof of concept that iPDCs can be used for preclinical assessment of new therapeutic agents.

del in testing new therapeutic agents for HGSC. We thus believe that our work offers a proof of concept that iPDCs can be used for preclinical assessment of new therapeutic agents. We have now accordingly edited the manuscript text to better convey this message to the reader, and to more clearly indicate the limitations of this study (lines 477-478, 480-481).

Regarding the Figure 2 experiment, it has weak connections to Figures 1 and 3, is based on analysis of small sample sizes, and has low significance of results.

The finding that combination of ATR inhibitors and ATX inhibitors activates CD8 T cells and activates CD11c-positive myeloid cells is an important observation.

Author response: Thank you for this important remark. We have improved the coherence of the text between the sections (lines 344-346, 354, 367-368, 399). Additionally, we have made the proof-of-concept nature of our studies more obvious, and that previous studies have had similar results, which our study confirms (lines 365, 367-368, 403-404).

The following are minor comments:

1. Line 119: Please describe in g rather than RPM as the type of centrifuge rotor is unclear.

Author response: Thank you for this important observation, and have now corrected the units into g.

2. Line 218: Please correct the typo where "and" is italicized.

Author response: We have corrected this formatting error.

3. Line 363: Please include the number as it currently shows ">XX months."

Author response: We thank the reviewer for this important observation, and have now added the months as their correct numbers

4. Line 484: Please correct the typo "respondes."

Author response: Thank you, we have corrected this error.

5. Data availability: While WES data may be unavoidable, scRNA-seq data should be deposited.

Author response: Data upload to GEO - add link when Wenqing has completed upload.

Reviewer #2 (Comments to the Author ):

This manuscript by Nagaraj et.al. presents a significant body of work (i) developing a novel patient derived organoid tumor model that incorporates an omental gel, (ii) validating the model, showing strong preservation of a diverse immune cell repertoire and correlating therapeutic response with patient response, (iii) using the platform to identify an ATR inhibitor/immunotherapy combination as a potential new therapeutic approach for ovarian cancer.

I view this paper as having many strengths and only a few weaknesses.

Major Concerns

1. PDC response to various therapies are tested (parp, ATRi etc), but response the primary therapy – carboplatin and taxol, is not. This would add to the paper to see the response correlation with patients response.

Author response: We thank the reviewer for this important suggestion. However, we carefully considered different options for making this correlation in terms of the most accurate clinical variables. Firstly, correlation to primary therapy outcome (RECIST 1.1) would be impossible due to that during 1st line treatment, >90% of the patients achieve complete response to the combination of surgery and 1st line chemotherapy (https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S235257892400119X). We also did not have serial measurements during chemotherapy to use blood biomarkers (e.g. Kelim score https://www.ejcancer.com/article/S0959-8049(23)00318-0/fulltext) to infer chemotherapy. PFS is also the most important response evaluation method recommended for 1st line ovarian cancer patients (https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1048891X24038714). However, approximately 40-50% of HGSC patients receive PARPi as 1st line maintenance therapy, significantly confounding the analysis when making correlations of PFS. However, for the chemotherapy resistant patient whom we had access to recurrent disease sample, we investigated the responses to platinum-based chemotherapy in iPDCs and showed that the iPDCs were indeed resistant to platinum chemotherapy in line with the clinical response (Figure 3B).

Due to these limitations, we decided to associate the responses to PARP inhibitors, using time on PARPi and PFS as the clinical outcome measures in patients who have received PARP inhibitors as 1st line maintenance. We show significant correlation between iPDC PARPi response and clinical responses of the patients correlate significantly (Figure 1G, H). We have now added a discussion about this rationale in the manuscript (lines 323-327).

2. The PDC response to ATRi/ATXi is interesting and an important finding with clinical implications. It would be nice to see this validated in a murine model or humanized PDX model.

Author response: We agree on the relevance of our finding with ATRi/ATXi inhibitors in ovarian cancer. Importantly, the fact that our results are in line with the Phase II clinical trial showing the efficacy of ATRi in ovarian cancer (https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38635934/) emphasizes the clinical relevance or our iPDC model. For the ATXi, we have previously published comprehensive mechanistic characterization in vivo on the effects of targeting the LPA pathway (https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/35552618/). Using genetically engineered, syngeneic mouse models of HGSC we show that ATXi reprogrammed the tumor immune microenvironment, extended host survival, and improved the effects of therapies that elicit protective responses driven by type I IFN. This again highlights the high relevance of our fully humanized iPDC model in testing new therapeutic agents for HGSC. We have now clarified and added clear references to the Chae et al to the manuscript (lines 406-409)

Minor –

1. For the correlation of PDC response and duration response to therapy it is unclear how many patients were tested – it would be nice to see the individual data points to know if results are skewed by the one outlier.

Author response: We thank the reviewer for pointing out issues in our figure presentation. The individual data points in Figure 1 panel G were denoted in the original version of the panel, but we have increased their visibility, as well as for the trendline as well. Additionally, we have improved the visibility of the revised Figure 1 panel E trendline as well to ensure no data is lost from the figures.

2. How long can the immune populations remain viable in this system – studies were from 4-7 days. It would be good to know how long viable immune cells are present.

Author response: We thank the reviewer for this comment. We have now clarified that the iPDC cell proportions depicted in Figure 2 panel A are obtained from experiment endpoints, and that the cells are viable, as the flow cytometry panel contains a viability dye (lines 728-730, Supplemental Figure 2 legend). To provide an additional intra-experiment data points of immune cell viability, we have added data on a new set of five patient samples and their immune cell proportion comparisons at day 0 and day 3/4, to further give an insight on immune cell proportion evolution during the iPDC experiments Supplemental Figure 2, panel F.

3. Would be nice to have some rationale for why ATRi and Wee1 inhibitors were chosen for the studies in figure 2 (there are lots of promising agents).

Author response: We thank the reviewer for raising this point. The treatments were based on our and others previous work investigating new treatment options for chemoresistant ovarian cancer. Previous clinical and preclinical work have suggested ATRi (Konstantinopoulos et al; https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-021-25904-w, Chae et al; https://doi.org/10.1158/2159-8290.Cd-21-1181) and Wee1 (Fu et al; https://doi.org/10.1200/JCO.22.00830) as options for treatment for therapy resistant ovarian cancer. Additionally, our previous work displays that ATRi and WEE1i can be also effective upon PARPi resistance in vitro (https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33514515/). We have added the rationalization to the manuscript text to increase the clarity of this rationale (lines 406-409).

4. Fig S4 A is important information but the label for the drugs used is not readable/text is too small.

Author response: Thank you for pointing out this issue. We have completely reformatted Supplemental Figure 4 panel A to improve the visibility of the labels.

5. Line 363 – reports patient responded for ‘xx’ months – this should be corrected.

Author response: Thank you for bringing this to our attention, we have now added the months as their correct numbers

6. C218_L is not explained anywhere in the text

Author response: We thank the reviewer for this important observation, and have now added a description and rationalization for C218_L in the text (lines 370-371)

7. In the discussion the paper mentions response to PERKi being tested, but this is not mentioned in the main results section. It is in the list of drugs tested and is in supplemental information. But if it is important enough to mention in the discussion it likely should be in the main paper.

Author response: We thank the reviewer for bringing this item for discussion, and have decided to remove discussion references to PERKi, as the response to this inhibitor is not significant enough to warrant singling it out amidst the high-throughput drug testing panel.

8. Im guessing this is an article formatting issue, but there is a lot of data jammed in just 3 figures. If room allows this would be better expanded into more figures and potentially including some of the supplemental information

Author response: We thank the reviewer for this important suggestion. We have now expanded the main figure count from three figures to five, and brought a total of five supplemental figure panels to the main figures. This has improved the flow and spacing of the figures, and created a better rhythm for the study.

Senior Editor Comments:

The novel human ex vivo experimental system presented is timely and of interest. Results provide proof-of-principle for the potential utility of this system to investigate combinations of targeted agents with immunotherapy. Please, modify some of the language in the abstract to emphasize the preliminary nature of the identified new combination treatment for HGSC, which requires further validation. In addition, all issues raised by the reviewers should be addressed, and some data moved from supplementary figures to main figures.

Author response: We thank the senior editor for their kind words. According to the comments by the editor and the reviewers, we have extensively edited the main figures, and included supplemental data in the main figures. We have additionally edited the abstract to be more in line with the findings and cohort size highlighting the proof of concept nature of our work.
